questions.question: DLNA (used in HAN standards) expands to:
questions.explanation: DLNA stands for Digital Living Network Alliance, a trade organization created by Sony, Intel, and Microsoft to connect cable-based and wireless networks for media sharing[cite: 2159, 2160, 2164].

questions.question: Which property best characterizes V2X?
questions.explanation: V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) communication follows a distributed architecture where content is widely distributed over the network and is not restricted to a single source[cite: 2174, 2178, 2183].

questions.question: Which set correctly captures smart-home functionality?
questions.explanation: Smart-home functionality encompasses multiple areas, including health monitoring, conservation of resources, and security and safety[cite: 2191, 2194, 2200].

questions.question: Which are valid types of sensing?
questions.explanation: In environments like smart parking, information sensing can be either stationary (fixed sensors detecting presence) or mobile (collecting info along a route)[cite: 2211, 2216, 2218].

questions.question: Which layers are part of the Zigbee stack?
questions.explanation: The Zigbee stack is built on the IEEE 802.15.4 standard and includes the Network and MAC layers[cite: 2224].

questions.question: Smart parking primarily reduces traffic ____.
questions.explanation: By shortening the parking search time for drivers, smart parking systems directly reduce traffic congestion and pollution[cite: 2233, 2234, 2239].

questions.question: About Home Area Network (HAN), consider: I) Multi-protocol gateway bridges non-IP to IP. II) Wired HAN is low cost. III) Wireless HAN makes implementation easy. Which of these is correct?
questions.explanation: All statements are correct: multi-protocol gateways bridge different device types, wired HAN (using existing power lines) is low cost, and wireless HAN simplifies implementation[cite: 2245, 2246, 2247].

questions.question: Which statements about smart health are true? I) Low-cost and portable II) Allows remote check-ups and diagnosis III) Effortless health monitoring
questions.explanation: Smart health is characterized by being low-cost and portable, enabling remote diagnosis, and allowing for effortless on-body health monitoring[cite: 2259, 2260, 2261, 2264, 2265].

questions.question: Phases of Intelligent Connected Vehicle (ICV) development include:
questions.explanation: ICV development has evolved from Phase-1 (based on 2G/3G) for basic infotainment to Phase-2 (based on 4G LTE or DSRC) for intelligent transportation services[cite: 2274, 2277, 2281].

questions.question: In the Ad-hoc domain of VANET, vehicles are:
questions.explanation: In the ad-hoc domain of VANET, the On-Board Units (OBUs) in vehicles are mobile, while Road-Side Units (RSUs) are static[cite: 2291, 2292, 2295].

questions.question: Domains of VANET include:
questions.explanation: VANET domains are categorized into in-vehicle, ad-hoc, and infrastructure domains[cite: 2305, 2306, 2308, 2310].

questions.question: At which data fusion stage are multiple classifier outputs combined?
questions.explanation: The decision level stage involves an ensemble of decisions where multiple classifier or sensor outputs are combined for a final result[cite: 2318, 2319, 2326].

questions.question: The Body & Brain Architecture consists of how many layers?
questions.explanation: Body & Brain is a three-layered in-vehicle networking architecture consisting of the Body (sensors), Nervous System (transport), and Brain (coordination/intelligence)[cite: 2337, 2341].

questions.question: Which statement about smart home infrastructure is correct?

options.b: It may include wired and/or wireless devices.

questions.explanation: Smart home infrastructure integrates various devices using both wired and wireless technologies for personalized and safe living spaces[cite: 2352, 2355].

questions.question: Intelligent Connected Vehicles (ICV) communication is based on:
questions.explanation: ICV communication is based on Dedicated Short Range Communication (DSRC) technology, using standards like IEEE 802.11p[cite: 2361, 2362, 2364].
